FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD cohort: 9,855 children from the USA with baseline (ages 9-10) and 2-year follow-up intelligence measures.

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal design with baseline and 2-year follow-up measurements of intelligence across screen time categories (watching, socializing, gaming).

FT_IC6.evidence: Full-text empirical paper with complete methods, results tables, and numeric outcomes (correlations and standardized betas for screen time impact on intelligence).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 MET: Multiple phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait outcome (intelligence). Time watching (r=-0.12 baseline, β=+0.12 follow-up), time socializing (r=-0.10 baseline, no effect follow-up), gaming (no correlation baseline, β=+0.17 follow-up). Other side is cognitive ability (intelligence quotient), an individual-trait variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: Pearson correlations at baseline (r=-0.12, r=-0.10), standardized regression coefficients at follow-up (β=+0.17, β=+0.12), controlling for genetic differences and socioeconomic status.

FT_IC4.evidence: Clear numeric results throughout: baseline correlations and 2-year standardized beta coefficients for screen time categories predicting intelligence. All results reported with effect sizes.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies: Paper includes baseline cross-sectional analysis (r correlations at ages 9-10) alongside longitudinal follow-up; any cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis disqualifies EC7.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Longitudinal design with phone/SM screen time as predictor and cognitive ability as outcome. Post-hoc sensitivity analysis notes that watching benefit disappears when parental education (vs. SES) is controlled, but main results clearly reported across multiple time points.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD cohort: 11,000+ children aged 9-11 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study with genetics, neuroimaging, and behavioral data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Mixed design: Cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. Methods state both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses performed; longitudinal analysis shows directional effect of white matter microstructure on ADHD scale and bi-directional effect between ADHD scale and STU.

FT_IC6.evidence: Full empirical paper with methods, results, and comprehensive neuroimaging and genetic data. Mediation analysis framework with multiple quantitative outcomes (FA correlations, mediation pathways).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 MET: Screen time utilization (STU) is phone/SM-specific variable paired with individual-trait outcomes. Fractional anisotropy (FA) in white matter tracts negatively correlated with both ADHD polygenic risk score and STU (inferior frontal-striatal tract, inferior frontal-occipital fasciculus, superior longitudinal fasciculus, corpus callosum). ADHD trait (behavioral scale) is individual-trait-level outcome. Mediation analysis: FA mediates association between ADHD polygenic risk and STU.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative mediation effects reported: FA values in multiple white matter tracts show negative correlations with ADHD polygenic risk and STU. Longitudinal directionality effects (white matter microstructure → ADHD scale and bi-directional ADHD scale ↔ STU) quantified.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results throughout: FA correlations with ADHD risk and STU, mediation pathways demonstrating shared neural links. Longitudinal and cross-sectional effect estimates reported.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies: Paper includes both cross-sectional analyses (FA-ADHD-STU correlations at baseline) and longitudinal analyses; any cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis disqualifies EC7.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Dual design (cross-sectional + longitudinal) with STU as phone/SM predictor, white matter microstructure and ADHD trait as individual-trait outcomes. Mediation and directionality analyses provide mechanistic evidence.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD cohort: 10,691 participants (5,107 girls) with useable baseline structural imaging from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study.

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional design only. Paper analyzes 'baseline structural imaging' without any longitudinal follow-up measurements. No temporal ordering or repeated measures reported.

notes: Paper excluded at FT-IC5 for cross-sectional design only. Although paper reports numeric results (β=0.107 for SMA-covariation association, β=0.117 for externalizing psychopathology association) and meets FT-IC1, the lack of longitudinal follow-up fails the temporal requirement at FT-IC5, triggering cascade-DEFERRED for all downstream criteria.
